Q1: I tried to be really disciplined about my study routine because I knew I couldn't afford to fall behind. I'd block out about 20 hours each week, usually in the evenings after work and on weekends. I'd write everything down in a notebook since that helps me process information better. The routine wasn't perfect, but I stuck to it almost religiously because I knew missing even one session could throw everything off given my limited resources.
Q2: Honestly, my pattern stayed pretty consistent throughout the semester. I couldn't really afford to let things pile up because the anxiety of having work hanging over me was worse than just doing it regularly. Deadlines did make me push a bit harder, but I tried to stay ahead because catching up felt impossible with everything else I had going on. Consistency was my survival strategy.
Q3: I logged into the LMS mainly to check for announcements and see if any new materials were posted. I also wanted to make sure I wasn't missing deadlines since my attendance was something I took seriously. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course, especially when I was feeling anxious about whether I was keeping up with everyone else.
Q4: Yeah, checking the LMS became like brushing my teeth - just something I did every morning before anything else. I think it happened because I was so worried about missing something important. After a few weeks, it was automatic. I'd grab my coffee, open my laptop, and check the LMS before even looking at anything else online.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually really valuable to me because I could learn from other students' perspectives and questions. The reading materials and any written resources were my go-to since I learn best through reading and writing things out. I also found the video lectures helpful, and I'd often take detailed notes while watching them. These resources let me engage at my own pace, which was crucial.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete as many assignments as I should have - my completion rate was pretty low. Sometimes I'd start them but get overwhelmed by anxiety and set them aside. The interactive quizzes I sometimes skipped because they felt less important than the main readings. I know I should have engaged more with those practice materials, but time and mental energy were always in short supply.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what would be on exams or what would help me understand the core concepts. If I could take notes and really process the information in writing, that made it feel productive. I also valued anything where I could interact with peers because those discussions helped solidify my understanding and made me feel less alone in struggling with the material.
Q8: Required activities had this weight to them that made my anxiety spike, honestly. Optional ones felt like a relief in some ways because there was no pressure, but that also meant I often didn't prioritize them. When I did engage with optional materials, it was usually because I genuinely wanted to understand something better, not because I had to. That made the learning feel different - more personal, less stressful.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded pretty much everything and studied from printed notes and PDFs constantly. I'd spend hours with my notebook rewriting concepts in my own words - that's really how I learn. I also used some free resources I found online to supplement what we covered in class. A lot of my actual learning happened away from my computer entirely.
Q10: Most of my deep learning happened when I was sitting with my handwritten notes, reviewing and rewriting them. I'd download a lecture video once, take extensive notes, and then never watch it again on the LMS - but I'd review those notes dozens of times. The LMS would just show one video view, but that material got hours of my attention offline.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a reading or download a PDF once, then work with it for days or even weeks afterward. My internet access is decent but I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study anywhere without worrying about connectivity. The LMS only captured that initial download, not the countless hours I spent with those materials afterward.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when multiple deadlines converged and my anxiety was through the roof. My resources are limited, so any unexpected expense or life stress hit my studies hard. During those times, I'd still log in and watch videos, but my brain wasn't really absorbing anything. The CBT sessions I had helped me recognize when I was spiraling and try to pull back.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, I'd find myself logging into the LMS and just... staring at the screen without really engaging. Competing responsibilities were constant - I'm 25 and I have obligations beyond school. The fatigue made it hard to do the deep reading and writing that actually helps me learn. I'd end up doing surface-level engagement just to feel like I was doing something.
Q14: Yeah, more times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about bills or other life stuff. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times and still not absorb it because my anxiety was too high. The LMS would log that I spent time with the material, but I wasn't really there mentally.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than anything else, which is why my completion rate was so low. The anxiety around them was intense - I'd worry so much about doing them perfectly that I'd freeze up and avoid them entirely. I knew they were important, but the fear of failing at them was sometimes stronger than the motivation to try. It's something I'm still working on.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and terrifying for me. They'd push me to finally engage with materials I'd been avoiding, but they also spiked my anxiety significantly. I'd often cram a lot of LMS activity into the days right before something was due. The structure helped me prioritize, but the pressure sometimes made my engagement more frantic than thoughtful.
Q17: Feedback was huge for me, though sometimes it took me a while to process it emotionally before I could use it constructively. Positive feedback made me feel like maybe I could actually do this, which motivated me to engage more. Critical feedback was harder - I'd need to sit with it, maybe talk through it in my CBT sessions, before I could approach the material again without feeling defeated.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to organize my studying around, which I desperately needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my 20 hours each week. But when the structure was too rigid or didn't allow for my read-write learning style, I struggled. I appreciated when there were multiple ways to engage with the same material.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because I could process my thoughts in writing and learn from peers. Having materials available for download was essential for my offline studying. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks - my task completion suffered because keeping track of everything while managing anxiety was overwhelming. Simpler, clearer expectations would have helped.
Q20: I think that was me sometimes, honestly. High activity can mean someone is trying really hard but struggling to actually complete things. Anxiety can make you engage with materials over and over without feeling confident enough to submit work. Or maybe they're learning in ways that don't translate to traditional assignments. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying or not learning.
Q21: That makes sense to me because so much learning can happen offline. Someone might download everything once, study intensively from printed materials, and ace the exam without ever logging back in. The LMS only captures digital interactions, not the hours spent with a notebook or discussing concepts with study partners. Low activity doesn't mean low effort or low learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if someone is deeply focused or completely zoned out. They miss all offline studying, emotional states, life circumstances, and the actual learning happening in someone's mind. They're a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and connect with the material. It's not just about showing up or clicking through things - it's about genuine curiosity and effort. For me, engagement looks like taking detailed notes, participating in discussions, and thinking about the material even when I'm not formally studying. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just a physical one.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay engaged despite my anxiety and limited resources. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes or the mental energy it took just to log in some days. It can't see that I attended nearly every session and really tried, even when my assignment completion didn't reflect that effort. My resilience and determination are invisible in the data.
